agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform must display itemized earnings breakdowns to drivers across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications (employee vs. independent contractor), regulatory regimes (mandatory transparency vs. works council gatekeeping vs. no requirement), and cultural expectations. The feature must be jurisdiction-aware, not one-size-fits-all.","bounded_context":"Driver Compensation & Earnings Transparency — sits at the intersection of Payroll, Driver Management, and Regulatory Compliance bounded contexts. Owns the read-side projection of earnings data; does NOT own the write-side calculation of pay, tips, or deductions.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Base Pay","definition":"The fixed compensation component per shift or per hour. For employees (California), this is hourly wage subject to minimum wage laws. For independent contractors (Indonesia), this is a per-task or per-delivery base rate set by platform agreement."},{"term":"Per-Delivery Bonus","definition":"Variable compensation tied to completing individual deliveries, potentially modified by surge pricing, distance, or time-of-day multipliers."},{"term":"Tips","definition":"Voluntary customer gratuities passed through to the driver. In California under AB5, tips must never be counted toward minimum wage obligations."},{"term":"Deductions","definition":"Amounts subtracted from gross earnings. For employees: taxes, benefits, garnishments. For contractors: platform fees, equipment rental, insurance opt-ins. The nature and legality of deductions varies drastically by jurisdiction."},{"term":"Earnings Breakdown","definition":"An itemized, human-readable decomposition of a driver's gross-to-net compensation for a given period, showing each component and deduction line item."},{"term":"Real-Time Earnings","definition":"Earnings data that reflects completed deliveries and accrued pay with minimal delay. Exact latency tolerance is unspecified in the requirement."},{"term":"Works Council (Betriebsrat)","definition":"A legally mandated employee representation body in Germany that has co-determination rights over compensation display systems. Must formally approve before rollout."},{"term":"AB5 Classification","definition":"California law (Assembly Bill 5) that classifies most gig workers as employees, granting them wage transparency rights, minimum wage protections, and itemized wage statement requirements under Labor Code §226."},{"term":"Wage Statement (California)","definition":"Under Labor Code §226, employers must provide itemized statements showing gross wages, total hours, piece-rate units, deductions, net wages, pay period dates, and employer identification. This is a legal artifact, not just a UI feature."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Earnings breakdown must be segmented by jurisdiction, applying the local legal classification (employee vs. independent contractor) to determine which line items are displayed and how they are labeled.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Displaying employee-style deductions to contractors (or vice versa) misrepresents the legal relationship and creates regulatory liability. The system is in a corrupt state if classification and display are mismatched.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver in California, the earnings breakdown MUST show components consistent with employee classification (hourly wage, tax withholdings, benefits deductions). Given a driver in Indonesia, it MUST show components consistent with contractor classification (task-based pay, platform fees). No cross-contamination of line item types."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"In California, the earnings breakdown must satisfy Labor Code §226 itemized wage statement requirements: gross wages earned, total hours worked, piece-rate or per-delivery units, all deductions, net wages, inclusive dates of the pay period, and employer legal name and address.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Failure to provide a compliant wage statement is a per-occurrence labor code violation with statutory penalties of $50 for initial and $100 for subsequent violations per employee per pay period, plus potential class action exposure.","testable_assertion":"Given a California-classified driver views their earnings breakdown, THEN the display includes all nine fields mandated by Labor Code §226, and no mandatory field is omitted or rolled up into an aggregate."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Tips must never be included in base pay calculations for California employee-classified drivers.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Counting tips toward minimum wage is a wage theft violation under California law. If violated, the system is producing incorrect legal compensation.","testable_assertion":"Given a California driver's earnings breakdown, the base pay line item does not include any tip amounts, AND the tip line is displayed separately, AND base pay independently meets or exceeds the applicable minimum wage rate multiplied by hours worked."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The Germany earnings display feature MUST NOT be rolled out to German drivers until formal works council (Betriebsrat) approval is obtained and documented.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deploying a compensation display system without Betriebsrat approval violates the Works Constitution Act (BetrVG §87). This is not a preference — it is a legal precondition that, if violated, can result in injunctions halting the feature entirely.","testable_assertion":"Given the Germany rollout flag is checked, THEN the system verifies that a works council approval record exists with a valid approval date before enabling the feature for any German driver. If no approval record exists, the feature returns a jurisdiction-unavailable state."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Deductions displayed must be specific, itemized, and labeled — never shown as a single aggregate 'total deductions' line without sub-items.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"While itemization is legally required in California, it is a business-quality decision in other jurisdictions. A VP could decide to show aggregates in Indonesia, though it would be poor practice.","testable_assertion":"Given any driver views their earnings breakdown, THEN each deduction is shown as a separate labeled line item (e.g., 'Federal Tax Withholding', 'Platform Service Fee'), not as a single opaque total."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Earnings data must reflect all completed deliveries for the current active period. No completed delivery may be omitted or delayed beyond the defined real-time threshold.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Showing stale or incomplete earnings data undermines the entire purpose of the transparency feature and, in California, could constitute a wage statement violation if the data shown is inaccurate at the time of viewing.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver has completed a delivery AND the system has confirmed delivery completion, THEN the earnings breakdown reflects that delivery's compensation components within the real-time latency window."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The earnings breakdown is a read-only projection. Drivers must not be able to modify, dispute, or trigger recalculation from the earnings display UI.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The earnings display is a view of calculated compensation. If it could trigger writes, it would violate the boundary between the Compensation Calculation context and the Transparency Display context, creating data integrity risks.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver views their earnings breakdown, THEN no write operations are exposed — no edit, dispute, or recalculate actions are available on the earnings display surface."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver classified as an employee in California completes a delivery","when":"They view their earnings breakdown","then":"They see itemized base pay (hourly), per-delivery bonus, tips (separate from base pay), and itemized deductions (taxes, benefits, etc.) that collectively satisfy Labor Code §226 requirements"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver classified as an independent contractor in Indonesia completes a delivery","when":"They view their earnings breakdown","then":"They see task-based base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and platform fee deductions labeled appropriately for a contractor relationship — no tax withholdings or employee benefit deductions are shown"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"The works council in Germany has NOT yet approved the compensation display feature","when":"A German driver attempts to access the earnings breakdown","then":"The feature is unavailable and the driver sees a jurisdiction-appropriate message explaining the feature is not yet active in their region"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The works council in Germany HAS approved the compensation display feature","when":"A German driver views their earnings breakdown","then":"They see an earnings breakdown compliant with both German labor law and the specific terms approved by the Betriebsrat"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A driver has completed 3 deliveries today and a 4th delivery is just marked complete","when":"They refresh or view their earnings breakdown","then":"All 4 deliveries and their associated compensation components are reflected within the defined real-time latency window"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A California driver's base pay for the period is below minimum wage before tips are added","when":"The earnings breakdown is generated","then":"The system flags the minimum wage shortfall in the breakdown AND does not mask the shortfall by rolling tips into base pay"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A driver relocates from Indonesia to California mid-pay-period","expected_behavior":"Earnings must be split at the reclassification boundary. Pre-move deliveries display contractor-style breakdown; post-move deliveries display employee-style breakdown with full §226 compliance. The system must not retroactively reclassify historical earnings."},{"scenario":"A California driver completes a delivery but the tip is added by the customer 2 hours later","expected_behavior":"The earnings breakdown initially shows $0.00 tips for that delivery. When the tip posts, the breakdown updates to reflect it. The base pay line must remain unchanged — the late tip must not alter any other line item."},{"scenario":"A delivery is completed but then reversed (customer refund, fraud detection)","expected_behavior":"The earnings breakdown must reflect the reversal as a separate deduction line item, not silently remove the original earning. The driver must see what happened and why their net changed."},{"scenario":"Germany works council approval is granted, then later revoked or suspended","expected_behavior":"The feature must be immediately disabled for German drivers. Any cached or offline earnings data should remain accessible but clearly marked as historical. No new earnings projections are shown."},{"scenario":"Platform fees in Indonesia change mid-shift","expected_behavior":"Deliveries completed before the fee change show the old fee; deliveries after show the new fee. The breakdown must never retroactively apply the new fee to earlier deliveries."},{"scenario":"A California driver works a shift with zero deliveries but is on-call","expected_behavior":"Under AB5 employee classification, the driver may be entitled to hourly wages for on-call time. The earnings breakdown must show base pay for hours worked even with zero per-delivery bonuses, not display an empty or zero-total breakdown."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High. California Labor Code §226 violations carry statutory penalties ($50-$100 per occurrence per pay period) with class action exposure across the entire California driver fleet. Incorrect implementation could generate seven-figure liability. Conversely, proper transparency can reduce wage disputes and associated legal costs.","operational":"Medium-High. The feature requires jurisdiction-aware configuration, a works council approval workflow gate for Germany, and real-time data pipelines from the delivery and payroll systems. Operational complexity scales with each new jurisdiction added. The Germany rollout is blocked until Betriebsrat approval, creating a phased deployment requirement.","user":"High positive. Drivers gain trust and satisfaction through pay transparency. In California, this is a legal right. In Indonesia, it is a competitive differentiator for driver recruitment and retention. Poor implementation (confusing labels, stale data, missing items) directly erodes driver trust.","risk":"High. Three distinct risk vectors: (1) Legal non-compliance in California creates direct financial penalties, (2) Betriebsrat violation in Germany can result in injunctions halting the feature and damaging the employer relationship, (3) Incorrect contractor/employee labeling in any jurisdiction could be used as evidence of misclassification in litigation.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers (California)","impact":"Legally entitled to this information. Feature directly fulfills a statutory right. Incorrect implementation harms them financially and legally."},{"group":"Drivers (Germany)","impact":"Cannot access feature until works council approves. Must be clearly communicated that the delay is procedural, not discriminatory."},{"group":"Drivers (Indonesia)","impact":"Receive transparency as a platform benefit, not a legal right. Feature is a retention and trust tool."},{"group":"Legal & Compliance Team","impact":"Must validate §226 compliance for California, Betriebsrat process for Germany, and contractor agreement alignment for Indonesia before each jurisdiction goes live."},{"group":"Works Council (Germany)","impact":"Has co-determination rights. Must be engaged as a stakeholder, not merely notified. Their approval is a hard gate."},{"group":"Product & Engineering","impact":"Must build jurisdiction-aware feature toggling, localized earnings schemas, and a works council approval workflow. Cannot ship a single global implementation."},{"group":"Finance / Payroll","impact":"Source of truth for earnings data. Must expose real-time or near-real-time data feeds. Any discrepancy between payroll calculations and displayed earnings creates audit and legal risk."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec provides clear jurisdiction context and high-level requirements, but several critical details are ambiguously defined: 'real time' has no latency specification, 'deductions' categories are not enumerated per jurisdiction, the German works council process has no defined workflow, and the interaction between this display feature and existing payroll systems is unspecified. The three-jurisdiction complexity multiplies ambiguity — each vague term resolves differently depending on the legal context.","high_confidence_areas":["California AB5 classification requires employee-style wage statements under §226","Tips must be separated from base pay in California","Germany requires Betriebsrat approval before rollout — this is a hard legal gate","Indonesia drivers are contractors with different display requirements","Earnings display must be read-only and jurisdiction-segmented"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact latency threshold for 'real time' — is it seconds, minutes, or end-of-delivery?","Which specific deduction categories apply in each jurisdiction","Whether the German works council approval is a one-time gate or requires ongoing re-approval for changes","How the earnings display feature interacts with existing payroll/payment systems","Whether California §226 compliance requires this feature to serve as the official wage statement or supplement a separate one","Currency and localization requirements per jurisdiction"],"assumptions":["California drivers are already correctly classified as employees in the platform's HR system","A payroll or compensation calculation system already exists and can provide the source data","The works council in Germany has not yet been engaged, so approval timeline is unknown","Indonesia has no additional provincial regulations beyond national law that would affect this feature"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real time' is undefined. For a California employee, delayed earnings data could constitute an inaccurate wage statement (§226 violation). For an Indonesian contractor, a 15-minute delay might be acceptable. The spec does not define latency thresholds, and the correct answer differs by jurisdiction.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers (all jurisdictions)","Legal & Compliance Team","Engineering"],"options":["Define real-time as 'within 60 seconds of delivery completion confirmation' uniformly","Define per-jurisdiction SLAs (e.g., California: 60s, Indonesia: 5min, Germany: per works council agreement)","Define real-time as 'before the driver can start their next delivery'"],"recommendation":"Define per-jurisdiction latency SLAs, with California having the strictest threshold due to legal exposure. Recommend 60 seconds for California, configurable per jurisdiction."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Deductions' is a catch-all term that means fundamentally different things across jurisdictions. For California employees: federal/state tax withholding, SDI, FICA, benefits premiums, garnishments. For Indonesia contractors: platform commission, equipment fees, insurance opt-ins. The spec does not enumerate which deduction categories exist, nor whether drivers can see deduction rates vs. just amounts.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers (all jurisdictions)","Payroll/Finance","Legal & Compliance Team"],"options":["Define a master deduction taxonomy per jurisdiction as part of the domain model","Allow each jurisdiction to configure its own deduction categories dynamically","Require legal review to enumerate permitted deduction display items per jurisdiction before implementation"],"recommendation":"Legal and compliance must enumerate the deduction taxonomy per jurisdiction before any implementation begins. This is not an engineering decision — it is a legal and domain modeling prerequisite."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The relationship between this earnings display and California's official §226 wage statement is unclear. Is this feature THE wage statement (legally binding document), or a supplementary real-time view alongside a separate official statement? This distinction affects data accuracy requirements, retention obligations, and legal liability.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers (California)","Legal & Compliance Team","Engineering"],"options":["This feature IS the §226 wage statement — must meet all statutory formatting and retention requirements","This feature supplements a separate official wage statement — can be more flexible but must not contradict the official one","Provide both: real-time view for transparency plus a formal §226-compliant statement at pay period end"],"recommendation":"Treat the real-time view as supplementary to a formal §226 wage statement issued at pay period end. This reduces legal risk on the real-time display while still providing transparency. Legal counsel must confirm this interpretation."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform must display itemized earnings breakdowns to drivers across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications (employee vs. independent contractor), regulatory regimes (mandatory transparency vs. works council gatekeeping vs. no requirement), and cultural expectations. The feature must be jurisdiction-aware, not one-size-fits-all.","bounded_context":"Driver Compensation & Earnings Transparency — sits at the

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this domain scenario carefully.

This is about a "driver earnings transparency" feature for a global logistics platform operating across three jurisdictions with very different legal frameworks.

Let me classify the primary blocker:

The scenario describes requirements across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different legal/regulatory frameworks. The key issue is that critical legal and regulatory details are NOT provided by anyone in the scenario:
- What specific AB5 compliance requirements apply to earnings display?
- What is the works council approval process/timeline in Germany?
- What are the specific deduction categories allowed per jurisdiction?
- What does "real time" mean precisely?

But wait - "real time" is vague (ambiguity), and the works council requirement creates a sequencing constraint. 

The real problem here is ambiguity rather than missing context. The requirements are stated but open to interpretation—"real time" could mean per-delivery, per-hour, or live updating; "deductions" varies dramatically across jurisdictions; and "base pay" means entirely different things for employees versus contractors. The works council approval process is mentioned but without specifics on timeline or procedure, creating multiple valid interpretations of what needs to happen and when.

This is not an engineering decision — it is a legal and domain modeling prerequisite."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The relationship between this earnings display and California's official §226 wage statement is unclear. Is this feature THE wage statement (legally binding document), or a supplementary real-time view alongside a separate official statement? This distinction affects data accuracy requirements, retention obligations, and legal liability.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers (California)","Legal & Compliance Team","Engineering"],"options":["This feature IS the §226 wage statement — must meet all statutory formatting and retention requirements","This feature supplements a separate official wage statement — can be more flexible but must not contradict the official one","Provide both: real-time view for transparency plus a formal §226-compliant statement at pay period end"],"recommendation":"Treat the real-time view as supplementary to a formal §226 wage statement issued at pay period end. This reduces legal risk on the real-time display while still providing transparency. Legal counsel must confirm this interpretation."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

trace.result: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform must display itemized earnings breakdowns to drivers across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications (employee vs. independent contractor), regulatory regimes (mandatory transparency vs. works council gatekeeping vs. no requirement), and cultural expectations. The feature must be jurisdiction-aware, not one-size-fits-all.","bounded_context":"Driver Compensation & Earnings Transparency — sits at the intersection of Payroll, Driver Management, and Regulatory Compliance bounded contexts. Owns the read-side projection of earnings data; does NOT own the write-side calculation of pay, tips, or deductions.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Base Pay","definition":"The fixed compensation component per shift or per hour. For employees (California), this is hourly wage subject to minimum wage laws. For independent contractors (Indonesia), this is a per-task or per-delivery base rate set by platform agreement."},{"term":"Per-Delivery Bonus","definition":"Variable compensation tied to completing individual deliveries, potentially modified by surge pricing, distance, or time-of-day multipliers."},{"term":"Tips","definition":"Voluntary customer gratuities passed through to the driver. In California under AB5, tips must never be counted toward minimum wage obligations."},{"term":"Deductions","definition":"Amounts subtracted from gross earnings. For employees: taxes, benefits, garnishments. For contractors: platform fees, equipment rental, insurance opt-ins. The nature and legality of deductions varies drastically by jurisdiction."},{"term":"Earnings Breakdown","definition":"An itemized, human-readable decomposition of a driver's gross-to-net compensation for a given period, showing each component and deduction line item."},{"term":"Real-Time Earnings","definition":"Earnings data that reflects completed deliveries and accrued pay with minimal delay. Exact latency tolerance is unspecified in the requirement."},{"term":"Works Council (Betriebsrat)","definition":"A legally mandated employee representation body in Germany that has co-determination rights over compensation display systems. Must formally approve before rollout."},{"term":"AB5 Classification","definition":"California law (Assembly Bill 5) that classifies most gig workers as employees, granting them wage transparency rights, minimum wage protections, and itemized wage statement requirements under Labor Code §226."},{"term":"Wage Statement (California)","definition":"Under Labor Code §226, employers must provide itemized statements showing gross wages, total hours, piece-rate units, deductions, net wages, pay period dates, and employer identification. This is a legal artifact, not just a UI feature."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Earnings breakdown must be segmented by jurisdiction, applying the local legal classification (employee vs. independent contractor) to determine which line items are displayed and how they are labeled.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Displaying employee-style deductions to contractors (or vice versa) misrepresents the legal relationship and creates regulatory liability. The system is in a corrupt state if classification and display are mismatched.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver in California, the earnings breakdown MUST show components consistent with employee classification (hourly wage, tax withholdings, benefits deductions). Given a driver in Indonesia, it MUST show components consistent with contractor classification (task-based pay, platform fees). No cross-contamination of line item types."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"In California, the earnings breakdown must satisfy Labor Code §226 itemized wage statement requirements: gross wages earned, total hours worked, piece-rate or per-delivery units, all deductions, net wages, inclusive dates of the pay period, and employer legal name and address.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Failure to provide a compliant wage statement is a per-occurrence labor code violation with statutory penalties of $50 for initial and $100 for subsequent violations per employee per pay period, plus potential class action exposure.","testable_assertion":"Given a California-classified driver views their earnings breakdown, THEN the display includes all nine fields mandated by Labor Code §226, and no mandatory field is omitted or rolled up into an aggregate."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Tips must never be included in base pay calculations for California employee-classified drivers.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Counting tips toward minimum wage is a wage theft violation under California law. If violated, the system is producing incorrect legal compensation.","testable_assertion":"Given a California driver's earnings breakdown, the base pay line item does not include any tip amounts, AND the tip line is displayed separately, AND base pay independently meets or exceeds the applicable minimum wage rate multiplied by hours worked."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The Germany earnings display feature MUST NOT be rolled out to German drivers until formal works council (Betriebsrat) approval is obtained and documented.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deploying a compensation display system without Betriebsrat approval violates the Works Constitution Act (BetrVG §87). This is not a preference — it is a legal precondition that, if violated, can result in injunctions halting the feature entirely.","testable_assertion":"Given the Germany rollout flag is checked, THEN the system verifies that a works council approval record exists with a valid approval date before enabling the feature for any German driver. If no approval record exists, the feature returns a jurisdiction-unavailable state."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Deductions displayed must be specific, itemized, and labeled — never shown as a single aggregate 'total deductions' line without sub-items.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"While itemization is legally required in California, it is a business-quality decision in other jurisdictions. A VP could decide to show aggregates in Indonesia, though it would be poor practice.","testable_assertion":"Given any driver views their earnings breakdown, THEN each deduction is shown as a separate labeled line item (e.g., 'Federal Tax Withholding', 'Platform Service Fee'), not as a single opaque total."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Earnings data must reflect all completed deliveries for the current active period. No completed delivery may be omitted or delayed beyond the defined real-time threshold.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Showing stale or incomplete earnings data undermines the entire purpose of the transparency feature and, in California, could constitute a wage statement violation if the data shown is inaccurate at the time of viewing.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver has completed a delivery AND the system has confirmed delivery completion, THEN the earnings breakdown reflects that delivery's compensation components within the real-time latency window."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"The earnings breakdown is a read-only projection. Drivers must not be able to modify, dispute, or trigger recalculation from the earnings display UI.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"The earnings display is a view of calculated compensation. If it could trigger writes, it would violate the boundary between the Compensation Calculation context and the Transparency Display context, creating data integrity risks.","testable_assertion":"Given a driver views their earnings breakdown, THEN no write operations are exposed — no edit, dispute, or recalculate actions are available on the earnings display surface."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A driver classified as an employee in California completes a delivery","when":"They view their earnings breakdown","then":"They see itemized base pay (hourly), per-delivery bonus, tips (separate from base pay), and itemized deductions (taxes, benefits, etc.) that collectively satisfy Labor Code §226 requirements"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A driver classified as an independent contractor in Indonesia completes a delivery","when":"They view their earnings breakdown","then":"They see task-based base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and platform fee deductions labeled appropriately for a contractor relationship — no tax withholdings or employee benefit deductions are shown"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"The works council in Germany has NOT yet approved the compensation display feature","when":"A German driver attempts to access the earnings breakdown","then":"The feature is unavailable and the driver sees a jurisdiction-appropriate message explaining the feature is not yet active in their region"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The works council in Germany HAS approved the compensation display feature","when":"A German driver views their earnings breakdown","then":"They see an earnings breakdown compliant with both German labor law and the specific terms approved by the Betriebsrat"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A driver has completed 3 deliveries today and a 4th delivery is just marked complete","when":"They refresh or view their earnings breakdown","then":"All 4 deliveries and their associated compensation components are reflected within the defined real-time latency window"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A California driver's base pay for the period is below minimum wage before tips are added","when":"The earnings breakdown is generated","then":"The system flags the minimum wage shortfall in the breakdown AND does not mask the shortfall by rolling tips into base pay"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A driver relocates from Indonesia to California mid-pay-period","expected_behavior":"Earnings must be split at the reclassification boundary. Pre-move deliveries display contractor-style breakdown; post-move deliveries display employee-style breakdown with full §226 compliance. The system must not retroactively reclassify historical earnings."},{"scenario":"A California driver completes a delivery but the tip is added by the customer 2 hours later","expected_behavior":"The earnings breakdown initially shows $0.00 tips for that delivery. When the tip posts, the breakdown updates to reflect it. The base pay line must remain unchanged — the late tip must not alter any other line item."},{"scenario":"A delivery is completed but then reversed (customer refund, fraud detection)","expected_behavior":"The earnings breakdown must reflect the reversal as a separate deduction line item, not silently remove the original earning. The driver must see what happened and why their net changed."},{"scenario":"Germany works council approval is granted, then later revoked or suspended","expected_behavior":"The feature must be immediately disabled for German drivers. Any cached or offline earnings data should remain accessible but clearly marked as historical. No new earnings projections are shown."},{"scenario":"Platform fees in Indonesia change mid-shift","expected_behavior":"Deliveries completed before the fee change show the old fee; deliveries after show the new fee. The breakdown must never retroactively apply the new fee to earlier deliveries."},{"scenario":"A California driver works a shift with zero deliveries but is on-call","expected_behavior":"Under AB5 employee classification, the driver may be entitled to hourly wages for on-call time. The earnings breakdown must show base pay for hours worked even with zero per-delivery bonuses, not display an empty or zero-total breakdown."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High. California Labor Code §226 violations carry statutory penalties ($50-$100 per occurrence per pay period) with class action exposure across the entire California driver fleet. Incorrect implementation could generate seven-figure liability. Conversely, proper transparency can reduce wage disputes and associated legal costs.","operational":"Medium-High. The feature requires jurisdiction-aware configuration, a works council approval workflow gate for Germany, and real-time data pipelines from the delivery and payroll systems. Operational complexity scales with each new jurisdiction added. The Germany rollout is blocked until Betriebsrat approval, creating a phased deployment requirement.","user":"High positive. Drivers gain trust and satisfaction through pay transparency. In California, this is a legal right. In Indonesia, it is a competitive differentiator for driver recruitment and retention. Poor implementation (confusing labels, stale data, missing items) directly erodes driver trust.","risk":"High. Three distinct risk vectors: (1) Legal non-compliance in California creates direct financial penalties, (2) Betriebsrat violation in Germany can result in injunctions halting the feature and damaging the employer relationship, (3) Incorrect contractor/employee labeling in any jurisdiction could be used as evidence of misclassification in litigation.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Drivers (California)","impact":"Legally entitled to this information. Feature directly fulfills a statutory right. Incorrect implementation harms them financially and legally."},{"group":"Drivers (Germany)","impact":"Cannot access feature until works council approves. Must be clearly communicated that the delay is procedural, not discriminatory."},{"group":"Drivers (Indonesia)","impact":"Receive transparency as a platform benefit, not a legal right. Feature is a retention and trust tool."},{"group":"Legal & Compliance Team","impact":"Must validate §226 compliance for California, Betriebsrat process for Germany, and contractor agreement alignment for Indonesia before each jurisdiction goes live."},{"group":"Works Council (Germany)","impact":"Has co-determination rights. Must be engaged as a stakeholder, not merely notified. Their approval is a hard gate."},{"group":"Product & Engineering","impact":"Must build jurisdiction-aware feature toggling, localized earnings schemas, and a works council approval workflow. Cannot ship a single global implementation."},{"group":"Finance / Payroll","impact":"Source of truth for earnings data. Must expose real-time or near-real-time data feeds. Any discrepancy between payroll calculations and displayed earnings creates audit and legal risk."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec provides clear jurisdiction context and high-level requirements, but several critical details are ambiguously defined: 'real time' has no latency specification, 'deductions' categories are not enumerated per jurisdiction, the German works council process has no defined workflow, and the interaction between this display feature and existing payroll systems is unspecified. The three-jurisdiction complexity multiplies ambiguity — each vague term resolves differently depending on the legal context.","high_confidence_areas":["California AB5 classification requires employee-style wage statements under §226","Tips must be separated from base pay in California","Germany requires Betriebsrat approval before rollout — this is a hard legal gate","Indonesia drivers are contractors with different display requirements","Earnings display must be read-only and jurisdiction-segmented"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact latency threshold for 'real time' — is it seconds, minutes, or end-of-delivery?","Which specific deduction categories apply in each jurisdiction","Whether the German works council approval is a one-time gate or requires ongoing re-approval for changes","How the earnings display feature interacts with existing payroll/payment systems","Whether California §226 compliance requires this feature to serve as the official wage statement or supplement a separate one","Currency and localization requirements per jurisdiction"],"assumptions":["California drivers are already correctly classified as employees in the platform's HR system","A payroll or compensation calculation system already exists and can provide the source data","The works council in Germany has not yet been engaged, so approval timeline is unknown","Indonesia has no additional provincial regulations beyond national law that would affect this feature"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Real time' is undefined. For a California employee, delayed earnings data could constitute an inaccurate wage statement (§226 violation). For an Indonesian contractor, a 15-minute delay might be acceptable. The spec does not define latency thresholds, and the correct answer differs by jurisdiction.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers (all jurisdictions)","Legal & Compliance Team","Engineering"],"options":["Define real-time as 'within 60 seconds of delivery completion confirmation' uniformly","Define per-jurisdiction SLAs (e.g., California: 60s, Indonesia: 5min, Germany: per works council agreement)","Define real-time as 'before the driver can start their next delivery'"],"recommendation":"Define per-jurisdiction latency SLAs, with California having the strictest threshold due to legal exposure. Recommend 60 seconds for California, configurable per jurisdiction."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Deductions' is a catch-all term that means fundamentally different things across jurisdictions. For California employees: federal/state tax withholding, SDI, FICA, benefits premiums, garnishments. For Indonesia contractors: platform commission, equipment fees, insurance opt-ins. The spec does not enumerate which deduction categories exist, nor whether drivers can see deduction rates vs. just amounts.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers (all jurisdictions)","Payroll/Finance","Legal & Compliance Team"],"options":["Define a master deduction taxonomy per jurisdiction as part of the domain model","Allow each jurisdiction to configure its own deduction categories dynamically","Require legal review to enumerate permitted deduction display items per jurisdiction before implementation"],"recommendation":"Legal and compliance must enumerate the deduction taxonomy per jurisdiction before any implementation begins. This is not an engineering decision — it is a legal and domain modeling prerequisite."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The relationship between this earnings display and California's official §226 wage statement is unclear. Is this feature THE wage statement (legally binding document), or a supplementary real-time view alongside a separate official statement? This distinction affects data accuracy requirements, retention obligations, and legal liability.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers (California)","Legal & Compliance Team","Engineering"],"options":["This feature IS the §226 wage statement — must meet all statutory formatting and retention requirements","This feature supplements a separate official wage statement — can be more flexible but must not contradict the official one","Provide both: real-time view for transparency plus a formal §226-compliant statement at pay period end"],"recommendation":"Treat the real-time view as supplementary to a formal §226 wage statement issued at pay period end. This reduces legal risk on the real-time display while still providing transparency. Legal counsel must confirm this interpretation."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
